problem.text: An even number of circles are nested, starting with a radius of $1$ and increasing by $1$ each time, all sharing a common point. The region between every other circle is shaded, starting with the region inside the circle of radius $2$ but outside the circle of radius $1.$ An example showing $8$ circles is displayed below. What is the **least number of circles** needed to make the total shaded area at least $2023\pi$?
problem.highlight: Find the least number of circles for shaded area ≥ 2023π.

viz_requirements.description: Nested concentric circles sharing a common tangent point at the bottom. Circles drawn back-to-front with alternating shaded/unshaded fills. Supports progressive circle drawing, ring focusing/pulsing, and a final zoomed-out view with many circles.

actions.description: Fade in radial glow behind circles
Show the common tangent point dot
Animate drawing a circle of given radius
Dim all rings except ring k
Restore all rings to normal opacity
Sequentially pulse a list of rings
Hide last radius label and common point label

nodes.title: Drawing the Circles
nodes.objective: Student understands the geometric setup: nested circles sharing a common point, with alternating shaded rings.
nodes.context_from_previous: None — this is the first act.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Introduce the common point and explain we will draw circles of increasing radius, all tangent at that point.
Draw circles radius 1 through 8, one or two at a time. Point out shaded vs unshaded as each pair appears.
Summarize: with 2n circles we get n shaded rings, each labeled k=1..n.

nodes.question_hint: Which rings are shaded in this pattern? (Test understanding of the alternating odd→even pattern.)

nodes.title: The Area Formula
nodes.objective: Student recalls/learns that the area of a circle is πr².
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has seen the nested circles and understands which rings are shaded.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Remind student of the circle area formula A=πr² with examples at r=1,2,3.

nodes.question_hint: What is the area of a circle with radius r? (Multiple choice: 2πr, πr², πr, 2πr²)
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Explain the difference between area (πr²) and circumference (2πr). Use a visual comparison.

wrong_path_acts.title: Area vs. Circumference
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student confused area with circumference on the quiz.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Compare circumference (1D, 2πr) vs area (2D, πr²). Memory trick: dimension matches the exponent.

nodes.title: Anatomy of a Shaded Ring
nodes.objective: Student can compute a single ring's area by subtracting inner circle from outer circle.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student knows A=πr² and which rings are shaded (odd→even radius pairs).

beat_outline.narration_hint: Focus on ring k=1 (between r=1 and r=2). Show the subtraction: outer area minus inner area.

nodes.question_hint: Fill in the outer circle area (π(2)² = ?) and inner circle area, then the ring area.

nodes.title: The General Formula
nodes.objective: Student derives A_k = π(4k-1) for the k-th shaded ring.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student computed ring k=1 area = 3π by subtraction.

beat_outline.narration_hint: For ring k: inner radius = 2k-1, outer radius = 2k. Set up the subtraction.

nodes.title: The Pattern Emerges
nodes.objective: Student sees that ring areas form an arithmetic sequence with common difference 4π.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has the formula A_k = π(4k-1) and verified it for k=1,2.

nodes.question_hint: What type of sequence is 3π, 7π, 11π, 15π? (arithmetic, geometric, fibonacci, neither)
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Review arithmetic sequences: constant difference between consecutive terms. Sum formula: n/2 * (first + last).

wrong_path_acts.title: Arithmetic Sequences Review
wrong_path_acts.objective: Student understands arithmetic sequences and their sum formula.
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student didn't identify the ring areas as an arithmetic sequence.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Define arithmetic sequence (constant difference). Show our sequence fits. Introduce sum formula S = n/2 * (a1 + an).

nodes.title: Adding It All Up
nodes.objective: Student sums the first n ring areas using the Gauss trick to get S = n(2n+1)π.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Apply the Gauss pairing trick: write sum forwards and backwards, add columns. Each pair = 4n+2.
Result: 2S = n(4n+2), so S = n(2n+1). Total shaded area = n(2n+1)π.

nodes.title: Solving the Inequality
nodes.objective: Student solves n(2n+1) ≥ 2023 to find n=32, so 2n=64 circles.
nodes.context_from_previous: Total shaded area = n(2n+1)π. Need area ≥ 2023π.

beat_outline.narration_hint: So n=32, and the number of circles is 2n=64.
